flashcards.front: What is a vanishing point in computer graphics?
flashcards.back: A vanishing point is a point on the horizon where parallel lines appear to converge in a perspective projection. It is the mathematical representation of how parallel lines recede into the distance in 3D-to-2D transformation.

In 1-point perspective, there is one vanishing point on the horizon. All lines perpendicular to the viewing plane converge to this single point, commonly used for looking down a hallway or railway tracks.

2-point perspective uses two vanishing points on the horizon line, typically placed at left and right. Vertical lines remain vertical while horizontal lines converge to either vanishing point, commonly used for drawing buildings at corners.

3-point perspective has three vanishing points: two on the horizon line and one either above or below it. It is used for viewing tall structures from above (third point below) or below (third point above), creating dramatic upward or downward views.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between vanishing points and the camera center?
flashcards.back: Vanishing points are formed by projecting rays from the camera center (projection reference point) through points at infinity in the 3D scene. The camera center, vanishing points, and image plane geometry determine the perspective transformation.

flashcards.front: Define the horizon line in perspective projection.
flashcards.back: The horizon line (also called the vanishing line) is a horizontal line on the image plane that passes through all vanishing points. It corresponds to the camera's eye level and separates the scene into above and below eye-level regions.

flashcards.front: How are vanishing points calculated in the image plane?
flashcards.back: For a line with direction vector d passing through point P, the vanishing point V is calculated by projecting the direction vector to infinity along the perspective transformation: V = P + t*d, where t→∞, transformed through the perspective projection matrix.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between perspective and orthographic projection regarding vanishing points?
flashcards.back: In orthographic projection, parallel lines remain parallel and there are no vanishing points. In perspective projection, parallel lines converge to vanishing points, creating depth perception similar to human vision.

flashcards.front: What is an ideal vanishing point?
flashcards.back: An ideal vanishing point occurs when parallel lines in 3D are parallel to the image plane, resulting in no convergence in the 2D projection. These lines remain parallel in the rendered image.

flashcards.front: How does the focal length affect vanishing point positions?
flashcards.back: The focal length (distance from camera center to image plane) determines the field of view and thus the spacing of vanishing points. Shorter focal lengths (wider angles) cause vanishing points to be closer together, creating more dramatic perspective distortion.

flashcards.front: What is meant by vanishing point at infinity?
flashcards.back: Vanishing points at infinity occur in orthographic projection where parallel lines never converge. In homogeneous coordinates, this is represented by w=0 in the perspective projection matrix, indicating parallel projection rays.

flashcards.front: How are vanishing points used in camera calibration?
flashcards.back: Vanishing points provide geometric constraints for camera calibration. By identifying orthogonal vanishing points (from three mutually perpendicular sets of parallel lines), one can recover camera intrinsic parameters like focal length and principal point.

flashcards.front: What is the vanishing point theorem in perspective geometry?
flashcards.back: The vanishing point theorem states that all vanishing points of lines parallel to a given direction in 3D lie on a line called the vanishing line. The vanishing line of a plane is the image of the line at infinity of that plane.

The principal vanishing point is the vanishing point corresponding to lines perpendicular to the image plane (z-axis direction). It is typically aligned with the image center in a standard camera setup and represents the direction the camera is facing.

flashcards.front: How does perspective transformation matrix relate to vanishing points?
flashcards.back: The 4x4 perspective projection matrix transforms 3D homogeneous coordinates to 2D image coordinates. When points at infinity (w=0) are transformed, the resulting coordinates give the positions of vanishing points in the image plane.
